Metadata.Case_Number: Civil Appeals Nos 21 to 24 of 2024; Suit No 418 of 2018; Suit No 475 of 2018
Metadata.Coram: Woo Bih Li JAD, Debbie Ong Siew Ling JAD and See Kee Oon JAD
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Appellate Division of the High Court of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Shree Ramkrishna Exports Pvt Ltd

Facts.Text: Shree Ramkrishna Exports Pvt Ltd is an India-incorporated company in the business of manufacturing and trading in jewellery and precious stones, including diamonds, with a sales office in Mumbai and a jewellery manufacturing factory in Sachin, India.
For approximately 15 years leading up to 2015, Shree Ramkrishna Exports Pvt Ltd supplied diamonds to the business operated by Michael Kriss and David Kriss.

In December 2014, Michael Kriss met Rahul Dholakia in Mumbai to discuss a proposed business collaboration between entities associated with the Kriss family and entities associated with Shree Ramkrishna Exports Pvt Ltd.

At the end of January 2015, Michael and David Kriss travelled to Mumbai to meet Govind Dholakia in connection with the proposed collaboration between the Kriss-related entities and the SRK-related entities.

From around 2015-04-01, entities related to Shree Ramkrishna Exports Pvt Ltd and entities related to the Kriss family began operating a business collaboration involving the supply of diamonds and jewellery to US-based companies associated with both groups.

Under the collaboration starting in 2015, SRK and its related companies supplied diamonds and various kinds of jewellery to US-based affiliated companies of SRK and JDM, with the goods invoiced to J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd.

From 2015, the goods supplied by SRK and its related entities for the collaboration were shipped directly to US-based companies affiliated with SRK and JDM, while invoices for those goods were issued to J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd in Singapore.
The goods supplied under the collaboration were not physically transported to or from J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd in Singapore, and were held in the United States for sale by US retailers.

Under the collaboration, SRK and The Jewelry Company supplied goods for the account of J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd, while JDM-related entities and TJC Jewelry, Inc sold those goods on consignment to US retailers such as Macy's, Zales and JCPenney.

Between 2015-09-15 and 2017-04-14, SRK issued invoices to J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd for goods supplied, later described collectively as the 42M Invoices, with total payments by J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd amounting to US$42,994,312.66.

Between 2016-09-02 and 2017-08-02, SRK issued 205 invoices to J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd for goods supplied, later described collectively as the 23M Invoices, with aggregate invoice amounts of US$23,400,456.25.

Between 2016-10-08 and 2017-08-01, The Jewelry Company issued invoices to J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd for goods supplied, later described collectively as the 2.2M Invoices, with aggregate invoice amounts of US$2,211,077.91.
By 2017-08-31, SRK and its related entities had supplied goods invoiced to J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd with total invoice amounts of US$66,394,768.91, comprising the 42M Invoices and the 23M Invoices.
On 2017-08-31, the business collaboration between SRK-related entities and JDM-related entities, conducted through J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd, came to an end.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd is contractually obliged to pay Shree Ramkrishna Exports Pvt Ltd and The Jewelry Company for goods supplied under invoices issued between 2015 and 2017, and what amounts are payable after accounting for prior payments.
Party_Details.Rule: In a sale of goods arrangement, where a supplier issues invoices to a named company for goods delivered for that company's account, the company is prima facie obliged to pay the invoiced price or the agreed price for those goods, barring any contrary agreement on pricing, capital treatment, or settlement mechanisms proven on the evidence.
Party_Details.Application: SRK and The Jewelry Company issued invoices to J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd for goods supplied for its account between 2015 and 2017, and J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd made substantial payments on earlier invoices while later invoices remained unpaid. The collaboration structure used J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd as the invoiced entity for goods shipped to US affiliates, and the goods were recorded in J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd's internal accounting records as purchases alongside purchases from unrelated suppliers. These features align with a sale of goods arrangement in which J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd bears payment obligations for the invoiced goods.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The business arrangement operated as a joint venture rather than a standard buyer-seller relationship. Within this structure, goods supplied were intended to be treated as equity contributions valued at cost. Consequently, the invoices do not create an immediate contractual payment liability for the full stated amounts. Instead, under restitutionary principles, the supplier is entitled to reasonable compensation based on cost-plus pricing, subject to counter-restitution for any prior overpayments made by the corporate vehicle.

Metadata.Case_Number: Civil Appeals Nos 21 to 24 of 2024; Suit No 418 of 2018; Suit No 475 of 2018
Metadata.Coram: Woo Bih Li JAD, Debbie Ong Siew Ling JAD and See Kee Oon JAD
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Appellate Division of the High Court of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd is a Singapore-incorporated company in the business of trading jewellery and precious stones, incorporated on 31 March 2015 with an issued share capital of US$100 comprising 100 ordinary shares.
On incorporation on 31 March 2015, J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd issued 50 shares to Shaileshkumar Manubhai Khunt and 25 shares each to Michael Bernard Kriss and David Miles Kriss.

A nominee resident director, Mr Ng Chee Wooi Michael, was appointed under a Resident Director Indemnity Agreement, and he was to act on the instructions of Mr Jim Goldsborough as the 'Authorized Person' designated by Shailesh and the Kriss Brothers.
Shaileshkumar Manubhai Khunt, Michael Bernard Kriss and David Miles Kriss were appointed as directors of J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd on 15 April 2015.
J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd was incorporated in Singapore on 31 March 2015 as a vehicle for the parties' collaboration.

In May 2015, JGJ opened a bank account with the Israel Discount Bank in New York, and the signatories were Amit, Ashish, Jim and the Kriss Brothers; any one of them could authorise payments to be made from the account.
The Goods were invoiced to J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd, a Singapore-incorporated company formed as a vehicle for the parties' collaboration.

Under the collaboration structure, SRK and TJCI supplied the Goods directly to the JDM Entities and TJCNY in the US and invoiced JGJ for these Goods; the JDM Entities and TJCNY then sold the Goods to third-party retailers on a consignment basis.
On 11 May 2016, S Goldi transferred US$500,000 to Shailesh, who transferred US$200,000 to JGJ on the same day.
On 13 May 2016, Shailesh transferred US$300,000 to JGJ.
On 19 May 2016, S Goldi transferred another US$200,000 to Shailesh, who transferred US$200,000 to JGJ on the same day.

In September 2016, KPMG, an Indian partnership and member of the KPMG international network, was engaged by JGJ to carry out an accounting exercise, and it withdrew in October 2017 citing difficulties in obtaining the required support and data.
Between 15 September 2015 and 14 April 2017, JGJ made payments totalling US$42,994,312.66 to SRK in respect of invoices later described as the 42M Invoices.
In May 2017, daily trial balances of JGJ for 2016 and from January to August 2017 were sent by e-mail to AT Adler, showing purchase accounts and payables.

In 2017, JGJ requested a comfort letter from SRK saying that payment by JGJ for the goods supplied to it could be deferred until JGJ regained sufficient cash flow, because JGJ's liabilities exceeded its assets and there would otherwise have been a going concern qualification.
The parties agree that the collaboration was officially terminated on 31 August 2017.
On 28 December 2017, TJCNY commenced action against the JDM Entities in New York to recover diamonds and jewellery inventory allegedly stored in the JDM Entities' vault at their New York office.

On 27 March 2018, the JDM Entities and JGJ commenced action in New York against the SRK Entities and Ashish, including claims for the recovery of the JDM Entities' alleged excess contributions of capital and payment of expenses, together with half of JGJ's profits earned during the term of the JV.
SRK issued 958 invoices for the Goods from September 2016 to August 2017, and 205 invoices remained unpaid, comprising the SRK Unpaid Sum of US$23,400,456.25.
TJCI issued invoices to JGJ between 8 October 2016 and 1 August 2017, and the unpaid sum was US$2,211,077.91.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd incurred payment obligations for goods invoiced by Shree Ramkrishna Exports Pvt Ltd and The Jewelry Company between 2015 and 2017, and whether any such obligations are affected by capital treatment, reconciliation mechanisms, or set-off arrangements within the parties' collaboration structure.
Party_Details.Rule: Where a company is consistently invoiced for goods supplied for its account and records those goods as purchases in its accounting records, the company is generally treated as the buyer and is obliged to pay the agreed or reasonable price, unless a separate binding arrangement clearly displaces payment liability or provides for a different settlement mechanism such as capitalisation or netting after reconciliation.
Party_Details.Application: J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd was the named recipient on invoices issued by SRK and The Jewelry Company for goods supplied under the collaboration, and it made substantial payments on earlier invoices while later invoices remained unpaid. Its internal trial balances recorded the goods from SRK and The Jewelry Company as purchases alongside purchases from unrelated suppliers, and its auditors were provided with these records. J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd sought a comfort letter from SRK in 2017 to address concerns about its ability to meet obligations, indicating that these balances were treated as payables. In correspondence in March 2018, J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd's counsel referred to amounts "due" to SRK and relied on set-off and reconciliation rather than denying liability.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: While the collaboration functioned as a joint venture, the corporate vehicle acted as the designated recipient and central clearinghouse for the goods. Internal accounting records, trial balances, and requests for financial comfort letters demonstrate that the vehicle treated these invoiced amounts as concrete payables. Even if the goods were characterized as capital contributions within the broader venture, the vehicle itself remains liable to account for the reasonable value of the goods received under the collaboration's financial framework.

Metadata.Case_Number: Suit No 475 of 2018; Civil Appeals Nos 23 and 24 of 2024
Metadata.Coram: Woo Bih Li JAD, Debbie Ong Siew Ling JAD and See Kee Oon JAD
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Appellate Division of the High Court of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Shaileshkumar Manubhai Khunt

Facts.Text: Shaileshkumar Manubhai Khunt is an Indian national resident in Hong Kong who worked for Shree Ramkrishna Exports Pvt Ltd from 2005 to 2007 and is a director of S Goldi (Asia) Limited, a Hong Kong-incorporated company that serves as a marketing arm of SRK.
On 2015-03-31, Shaileshkumar Manubhai Khunt was issued 50 shares in J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd, representing 50% of its share capital.
On 2015-04-15, Shaileshkumar Manubhai Khunt was appointed as a director of J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd.
On 2016-05-11, S Goldi (Asia) Limited transferred US$500,000 to Shaileshkumar Manubhai Khunt, who then transferred US$200,000 to J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd on the same day.
On 2016-05-13, Shaileshkumar Manubhai Khunt transferred US$300,000 to J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd.
On 2016-05-19, S Goldi (Asia) Limited transferred US$200,000 to Shaileshkumar Manubhai Khunt, who then transferred US$200,000 to J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd on the same day.

In early 2018, Shaileshkumar Manubhai Khunt did not sign certain directors' resolutions of J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd, including resolutions dated 2018-01-22 and 2018-02-15 authorising legal proceedings against TJC Jewelry, Inc, Shree Ramkrishna Exports Pvt Ltd and The Jewelry Company.
On 2018-04-19, Shaileshkumar Manubhai Khunt did not attend the annual general meeting of J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd at which the revised financial statements for 2016 were adopted.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether decisions by the Kriss brothers in managing J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd, including passing resolutions to commence litigation and making specific adjustments to the company's financial statements, resulted in outcomes that were commercially prejudicial to Shaileshkumar Manubhai Khunt in his capacity as a shareholder.
Party_Details.Rule: In minority shareholder protection, the conduct of those in control of a company is assessed to determine if it departs from standards of fair dealing and causes prejudice to the shareholder's interests in that capacity, having regard to the company's constitution and any proven understandings among the participants.
Party_Details.Application: Shaileshkumar Manubhai Khunt held 50% of the shares in J. G. Jewelry Pte Ltd and was a director. After the collaboration ended, the Kriss brothers and the resident director passed resolutions authorising litigation against SRK-related entities and approved revised financial statements for 2016 that included four significant adjustments. Shailesh did not sign these resolutions and did not attend the meeting adopting the revised statements. The adjustments affected reported payables and balances with SRK and the Kriss brothers, altering the financial position of the company in a way that impacted his shareholding.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: A nominee shareholder possesses the standing to pursue a minority oppression claim. While claims relating to general corporate wrongs fall outside this scope, the unilateral execution of unjustified adjustments to the company's financial statements by majority directors constitutes commercially unfair conduct. Such actions unfairly prejudice the minority shareholder's position by altering reported balances and payables. The appropriate equitable remedy is the mandatory restatement of the financial accounts to remove the improper adjustments. However, the broader oppression claims regarding corporate management are dismissed.
